coredata.dc:title: Smartphone-assisted ratiometric fluorescence sensor for sensitive and portable α-glucosidase activity detection and inhibitor screening
coredata.dc:creator: Cao, Hui-Ting
Zhao, Tiansheng
Liu, Wei
Xu, Cai-Ying
Liao, Yi-Jing
Yan, Xi-Luan
Mai, Xi
Li, Na
coredata.dc:description: α-Glucosidase (α-Glu) is an enzyme that plays an essential role in the digestion of carbohydrates, and its activity detection and inhibitor screening are crucial for the therapy of diseases and development of new drugs. In this study, α-Glu can catalyze the hydrolysis of α-arbutin to produce hydroquinone (HQ), and HQ can regulate the in-situ synthesis of polymer carbon dots (PCDs) with ethylenediamine (EDA). PCDs and tetraphenylporphyrin tetrasulphonic acid (TPPS) are separately designed as green and red fluorescent sensing units. With the increase of α-Glu concentration, the number of PCDs is gradually increased, resulting in enhancement of green fluorescence and quenching of red fluorescence. Conversely, when α-Glu inhibitors (AGIs) are present, the activity of α-Glu is inhibited, leading to weak green fluorescence and strong red fluorescence. Thus a ratiometric fluorescence sensor was designed for the determination of α-Glu activity and screening of AGIs. The developed ratiometric sensor exhibits a linear relationship between fluorescence intensity ratios and α-Glu concentrations (0.01 to 0.04 U/mL) with limit of detection of 0.184 mU/mL, which is much lower than the single fluorescence model. In addition, rapid and portable detection of α-Glu can be achieved using a smartphone with a color picking software installed as a signal detection device, which greatly reduces the cost and time of detection and provides a promising method for qualitative identification and quantitative analysis of α-Glu in the resource-limited areas. Moreover, the method is very promising for high-throughput screening AGIs from natural products.
coredata.dcterms:subject: α-Glucosidase
Inhibitors
Ratiometric fluorescence
Polymer carbon dots
Smartphone

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Construction of the sensing platform for smartphone-assisted α-Glu detection
Determination of α-Glu in real samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China
Natural Science Foundation of Jiangxi Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We acknowledge the funding supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 81860610 , 82360857 ) and Jiangxi Provincial Natural Science Foundation ( 20224BAB216116 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Schematic illustration of the smartphone-assisted ratiometric fluorescence sensor for α-Glu activity detection and inhibitor screening. Fluorescence (B) and UV–vis absorption (C) spectra of HQ (dash line), EDA (dot line), and HQ + EDA (solid line) solutions after heating in a water bath at 80 °C for 30 min. Concentrations: HQ (500 μM), EDA (250 mM). The insets in (B, C) are photographs of the corresponding solutions under UV and natural light, respectively. (D) FT-IR spectra of HQ, EDA, and PCDs. (E) TEM images of PCDs. (F) Diameter size distribution curves of PCDs. (G) Fluorescence spectra of PCDs prepared at different concentrations of HQ (0, 25, 50, 75, 100, 150, 200, 250, 300, 400, and 500 μM). Concentrations: EDA (250 mM). The inset shows the photographs of the corresponding solutions under 365 nm UV light.

Fluorescence (A) and UV–vis absorption (B) spectra of α-Glu (a), α-arbutin (b), α-Glu + α-arbutin (c), α-arbutin + EDA (d), and α-Glu + α-arbutin + EDA (e). Concentrations: α-arbutin (200 μM), EDA (150 mM), and α-Glu (0.025 U/mL). The insets in (A) and (B) represent corresponding solution photographs under 365 nm UV and natural light, respectively. (C) CIE chromaticity coordinates plot of PCDs (a) and TPPS (b). (D) Emission spectra of PCDs and TPPS at the excitation wavelength of 400 nm. The inset shows photographs of solutions of PCDs (left) and TPPS (right) irradiated by a 365 nm UV lamp. Concentrations: PCDs (3.6 mg/mL), and TPPS (8 μM).

The fluorescence spectra of the single fluorescence mode (A) and ratiometric fluorescence mode (D) after addition of different concentrations of α-Glu (0, 0.001, 0.005, 0.01, 0.015, 0.017, 0.02, 0.03, and 0.04 U/mL). Concentrations: α-arbutin (200 μM), EDA (150 mM), and TPPS (8 μM). The insets are photographs of the corresponding solutions under 365 nm UV light. (B) and (E) are linear relationships between F/F 0, F 510/F 650 and α-Glu concentration of 0.01–0.04 U/mL, respectively. (C) and (F) are CIE corresponding chromaticity diagrams of single fluorescence and ratiometric fluorescence sensing systems, respectively.

(A) The process of picking up the color of a sample and obtaining RGB values by a smartphone. (B) Photographs of solutions with different concentrations of α-Glu in the system under 365 nm UV light (right) and photographs of solution colors captured by the smartphone with color picking software (left). (C) Linear relationship between α-Glu concentration at concentrations of 0.005–0.02 U/mL and RGB ratio (R/B).

(A) Results of the selectivity test. (B) Fluorescence spectra of the system after addition of α-arbutin and different concentrations of α-Glu to normal mouse serum under pH 7.3 for 30 min in a water bath at 37 °C, and then adding EDA and TPPS and reacting at 80 °C for 15 min. (C) Linear relationship between α-Glu concentration (0.002–0.05 U/mL) and fluorescence ratio (F 510/F 650) in the real sample. Fluorescence spectra of the single fluorescence system (D) and ratiometric fluorescence sensing system (E) after the addition of different concentrations of acarbose from 0.4−32 mM. (F) S-type calibration curve of the concentration of acarbose versus the inhibition rate.

head.ce:title: Smartphone-assisted ratiometric fluorescence sensor for sensitive and portable α-glucosidase activity detection and inhibitor screening

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmaceutical Science, Nanchang University, Nanchang 330006, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmaceutical Science, Nanchang University, Nanchang 330006, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: China Automotive Technology & Research Center Co., Ltd, Tianjin 300300, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: China Automotive Technology & Research Center Co., Ltd

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: China Automotive Technology & Research Center Co., Ltd, Tianjin 300300, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: School of Pharmaceutical Science, Nanchang University, Nanchang 330006, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: α-Glucosidase (α-Glu) is an enzyme that plays an essential role in the digestion of carbohydrates, and its activity detection and inhibitor screening are crucial for the therapy of diseases and development of new drugs. In this study, α-Glu can catalyze the hydrolysis of α-arbutin to produce hydroquinone (HQ), and HQ can regulate the in-situ synthesis of polymer carbon dots (PCDs) with ethylenediamine (EDA). PCDs and tetraphenylporphyrin tetrasulphonic acid (TPPS) are separately designed as green and red fluorescent sensing units. With the increase of α-Glu concentration, the number of PCDs is gradually increased, resulting in enhancement of green fluorescence and quenching of red fluorescence. Conversely, when α-Glu inhibitors (AGIs) are present, the activity of α-Glu is inhibited, leading to weak green fluorescence and strong red fluorescence. Thus a ratiometric fluorescence sensor was designed for the determination of α-Glu activity and screening of AGIs. The developed ratiometric sensor exhibits a linear relationship between fluorescence intensity ratios and α-Glu concentrations (0.01 to 0.04 U/mL) with limit of detection of 0.184 mU/mL, which is much lower than the single fluorescence model. In addition, rapid and portable detection of α-Glu can be achieved using a smartphone with a color picking software installed as a signal detection device, which greatly reduces the cost and time of detection and provides a promising method for qualitative identification and quantitative analysis of α-Glu in the resource-limited areas. Moreover, the method is very promising for high-throughput screening AGIs from natural products.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Diabetes is a group of metabolic diseases characterized by hyperglycaemia, which is one of the most prevalent diseases. It is reported that there are 424.9 million people with diabetes worldwide in 2017, which will be increased to 628.6 million by 2045 [1]. The long-term presence of diabetes can lead to chronic damage and dysfunction of various tissues [2]. Many drugs have been widely explored to treat diabetes, among which α-glucosidase (α-Glu) inhibitor (AGI) is one of the effective drugs for diabetes. α-Glu can release glucose from the non-reducing end of α-glucoside, oligosaccharide, and glucan by cleaving the α-1,4 glycosidic bond [3,4]. The activity of α-Glu is not merely closely related to type-2 diabetes but also an important indicator of male infertility. Meanwhile, α-Glu can be selected as a drug target for the treatment of diabetes, providing a new avenue for the development of anti-diabetic drugs. AGIs can significantly lower blood glucose levels and control hyperglycemia associated with type-2 diabetes by slowing the absorption of carbohydrates in the intestines [5]. However, clinically used AGIs, such as voglibose and acarbose, can lead to serious intestinal discomfort. Therefore, detecting the α-Glu activity and screening AGIs with excellent α-Glu suppression effect and few side effects is of great significance.

The most common methods for AGIs screening are in vivo hyper-glycemic animal models and in vitro experiments using p-nitrophenyl-α-D-glucopyranoside (pNPG) as the substrate, which show shortcomings of expensive testing cost, long experimental time, and low sensitivity. Considerable methods have been established to overcome these drawbacks, including colorimetry [6,7], chromatography [8,9], electrochemistry [10,11], and surface plasmon resonance (SPR) [12,13]. While many of these methods can meet the sensitivity requirements, they often need expensive, bulky instruments, or are easily disturbed by environmental factors. Fluorescence methods for the analysis of enzymes have received increasing attention in recent years because of their rapid response, high sensitivity and simplicity of operation [14–16]. However, a single fluorescent signal is susceptible to external factors and is difficult to achieve the required accuracy, especially for highly precise assays [17,18]. Ratiometric fluorescence analysis can produce a correction similar to the internal standard signal by measuring two or more response signals simultaneously, thus overcoming the interferences and making the results more accurate and reliable [19,20].

So far, only a few ratiometric fluorescence methods have been introduced to α-Glu determination and AGIs screening. Most of these reports are built based on oxidation–reduction reactions between ascorbic acid (one product of α-Glu) and oxidizing substances, such as CoOOH [21], MoO2 [22,23], dopamine [24], Fe3+ [25], and Ce3+ [26]. As reported, the methods based on ascorbic acid are easily interfered by other reductive substances (e.g·H2O2, cysteine, glutathione). Besides, Su’s team synthesized N-doped carbon dots and fluorescence copolymer at the participation of hydroquinone (another product of α-Glu) for determination of α-Glu activity and acarbose [27]. However, the utilization of two kinds of nanomaterials makes the whole detection process more laborious and complex. Smartphones is a portable and easy-to-use tool with many advanced functions that can be achieved by installing applications. Therefore, it has been widely used in the on-site analysis of different targets based on the ratiometric fluorescence strategy [28–30]. The key to applying smartphones for on-site analysis is that the designed ratiometric fluorescence probe can exhibit noticeable color changes to the target analyte in the sensing system. Thus, it is urgent to design simple, selective, and sensitive ratiometric fluorescent α-Glu detection and AGIs screening method.

In general, fluorescent probes are crucial for development of ratiometric fluorescence methods, in which nanomaterials with different fluorescent colors are considered to be one of the most popular fluorescent probes, such as noble metal nanoclusters [31], semi-conductor quantum dots (QDs) [32], carbon quantum dots (CQDs) [33,34]. Although these probes have exhibited satisfactory fluorescence properties, the complicated and time-consuming preparation process, high temperature and pressure synthesis condition, and even the introduction of toxicity have limited their further applications. Polymeric carbon dots (PCDs) have received much attention in the past few decades due to their unique properties such as mild synthetic conditions, excellent fluorescence properties, high quantum yield and excellent photostability [35–37], which are now widely used for the analysis of biological molecules.

Herein, a ratiometric fluorescent method is proposed by combining in-situ synthetic fluorescent PCDs and commercially available tetraphenylporphyrin tetrasulfonic acid hydrate (TPPS) for α-Glu detection and AGIs screening. As shown in Fig. 1 A, the hydroquinone (HQ) can be released from α-arbutin when catalyzed by α-Glu, which can react with ethylenediamine (EDA) to form the green fluorescent PCDs. The red fluorescence of TPPS can be quenched with introduction of PCDs by inner filter effect (IFE). With increasing concentration of α-Glu, the number of PCDs is gradually increased, leading to enhanced green fluorescence and quenched red fluorescence. Meanwhile, the fluorescence color of the solutions varies from red to green, which can be processed by smartphone combined with color picking software. On the contrary, when AGIs are present with α-Glu, the activity of α-Glu is suppressed, and the hydrolysis of α-arbutin is inhibited to produce HQ, resulting in weak green fluorescence and strong red fluorescence. Therefore, a ratiometric fluorescence sensor is developed for α-Glu activity detection and inhibitor screening. The constructed method can be used for rapid, high-throughput AGIs screening by smartphones with color picking software, which can be further confirmed by the fluorometer.

α-Glucosidase (α-Glu), α-arbutin, hydroquinone (HQ), ethylenediamine (EDA), acarbose, and p-nitrophenyl-α-D-glucopyranoside (pNPG) were obtained from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Bovine serum albumin (BSA) was purchased from Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Glutathione (GSH), acetylcholinesterase (AChE), glucose (Glc), glutamate (Glu), and L-histidine (L-His) were bought from Adamas-beta Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The rhodiola rosea was purchased from the local pharmacy. All metallic and non-metallic salts were of analytical reagent grade and provided by Titan Scientific Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm) was used throughout the experiments to prepare the sample solutions.

The fluorescence spectra were measured on a Hitachi F-2700 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan). The ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were recorded using the Hitachi UH5300 spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan). The Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) were determined at the Thermo Fisher Nicolet iS5 spectrometer (New York, USA). The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were taken on a FEI Talos F200X G2 (USA). The scanning electron microscope (SEM) images were taken on a Hitachi Regulus 8100 (Japan). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurement was performed on a Panalytical Empyrean (Dutch). Solutions were blended on a Titan vortex mixer T1 (Shanghai, China).

A facile and effective synthetic route was designed for the preparation of PCDs [38]. First, HQ (10 mL, 2 mM) was added to the HAc-NaAc buffer (89.2 mL, pH 7.3), and then EDA (0.8 mL, 12.5 M) was added. Afterwards, the solution was heated in an 80 °C water bath for 30 min, after which the solution changed from light red to golden yellow. The final solution was treated in a dialysis bag (1000 Da, MWCO) for approximately 24 h to eliminate the precursor molecules. Finally, the collected PCDs were freeze-dried and weighed, then stored in the refrigerator at 4 °C for future use.

Firstly, α-arbutin (50 µL, 2 mM) was dissolved in HAc-NaAc buffer (100 µL, pH 7.3). Afterwards, 50 µL of different concentrations of α-Glu solution was added. The above solutions were mixed sufficiently and reacted in a water bath at 37 °C for 30 min. Secondly, EDA (6 µL, 12.5 M) and HAc-NaAc buffer (294 µL, pH 7.3) were added. After these samples were heated in a water bath at 80 °C for 15 min, the yellowish PCDs solution was collected. Finally, TPPS (1 mM, 4 μL) was added to each sample, shaken well and the fluorescence spectra were measured under excitation of 400 nm. The error bars in the work indicate the three parallel standard deviations of measurements.

Firstly, 1.0 g rhodiola rosea powder was extracted by 50 mL, 50 % ethanol for 30 min by ultrasound. After extraction and centrifugation, the ethanol was evaporated from the filtrate by spin evaporation and the residue was dried for further analysis.

To explore α-Glu inhibitors, the HAc-NaAc buffer (100 µL, pH 7.3) was mixed with α-Glu (20 µL, 0.5 U/mL), α-arbutin (40 µL, 2.5 mM) and 40 µL of different concentrations of inhibitors (acarbose, rhodiola rosea, gallic acid, quercetin, and epicatechin). After 30 min of reaction, EDA (6 µL, 12.5 M) and HAc-NaAc buffer (290 µL, pH 7.3) were added and shaken well, and the above solutions were placed in a water bath at 80 °C. After 15 min, TPPS (1 mM, 4 μL) was added to the samples. The fluorescence spectra were collected at an excitation wavelength of 400 nm.

A simplified synthetic route was designed for the preparation of PCDs using HQ and EDA (Fig. S1). As shown in Fig. 1B and 1C, when HQ is mixed and heated with EDA, a golden yellow solution with green fluorescence is obtained (the inset in Fig. 1B and 1C). However, the solution of HQ or EDA alone shows no obvious absorbance or fluorescence, which suggests the successful synthesis of green fluorescence PCDs. The FT-IR spectra were measured to further characterize the functional groups and chemical composition of PCDs (Fig. 1D). The peak of PCDs around 3400 cm−1 is caused by the stretching vibrations of O-H and N-H on their surfaces [39,40], the peak around 1600 cm−1 is ascribed to the bending vibrations of N-H [41], and the peak around 1300 cm−1 is corresponding to the stretching vibrations of C-O and C-N [42]. The FT-IR results show that there are abundant polar functional groups on the surface of the prepared PCDs, which endows PCDs with excellent hydrophilicity and dispersibility. The TEM measurement shows a mono-dispersed and quasi-spheroidal structure of the prepared PCDs (Fig. 1E). And their particle distribution size is in the range of 3 to 12 nm with an average size of about 6.1 nm (Fig. 1F). The aggregated PCDs are observed from the SEM measurement, which may be because the size of PCDs is too small to measure because of limited magnification of SEM (Fig. S2). Furthermore, the XRD image of PCDs is shown in Fig. S3, in which the broad diffraction peak at 2θ of 25.7° indicates the amorphous nature [43]. However, as shown in Fig. S4A, unlike other carbon-based nanomaterials, the emission wavelength of PCDs is almost independent of the excitation wavelength in the range of 360 nm to 460 nm. The fluorescence signal is strongest when the excitation wavelength is 400 nm (Fig. S4A). Thus, the excitation wavelength of 400 nm and the emission wavelength of 510 nm are chosen for the following experiments (Fig. S4B). And under the UV light irradiation, the golden yellow PCDs solution can emit bright green fluoresce (inset in Fig. S4B). The above data further confirm the successful synthesis of PCDs with HQ and EDA under a very mild condition. As seen in Fig. 1G, the fluorescence signal of the PCDs solutions show a gradual increase with the increasing concentration of HQ. Meanwhile, the color of the solution gradually changed from colorless to green under the irradiation of UV light. The concentration of HQ plays an important role during the PCDs synthesis process, which provides the basis on which a HQ-based sensing strategy can be built.

As shown in Fig. S5, the HQ is released from the α-Glu-catalyzed hydrolysis of α-arbutin, which can react with EDA to produce PCDs. The feasibility of the α-Glu assay was evaluated by fluorescence and UV–vis absorption spectroscopy. Both fluorescence emission at 510 nm and absorbance at 420 nm can be observed when the EDA solution is added to a buffer mixture of α-Glu and α-arbutin (curve e and inset e in Fig. 2 A and 2B). Meantime, when only α-Glu (curve a and inset a), α-arbutin (curve b and inset b), α-Glu and α-arbutin (curve c and inset c), or α-arbutin and EDA (curve d and inset d) are present in the buffer, there is no fluorescence signal or new absorbance peak. The above results demonstrate that the fluorescence can be observed when α-Glu, α-arbutin and EDA are present, which provides the basis for the feasibility of using PCDs for α-Glu quantification.

In addition, a ratiometric fluorescence sensor was constructed for α-Glu detection using the PCDs and TPPS. As mentioned above, the PCDs with green fluorescence can be obtained in the presence of α-Glu. Meanwhile, a dye TPPS with red fluorescence was introduced to develop a ratiometric fluorescence sensor. Interestingly, when TPPS is mixed with PCDs, the red fluorescence of TPPS is decreased (Fig. S6A). Considering the excitation spectrum of TPPS is overlapped with the absorbance of PCDs, the fluorescence quenching of TPPS can be attributed to IFE effect (Fig. S6B) [44,45]. Thus, the green fluorescence of PCDs is increased and the red fluorescence of TPPS is decreased with α-Glu, providing a prerequisite for the successful construction of ratiometric fluorescence sensor. As can be seen from the Commission Internationale de l’Eclairage (CIE) chromaticity diagram (Fig. 2C), the fluorescence color of PCDs (0.2044, 0.5207) and TPPS (0.5684, 0.2999) are quite a different, indicating that the proposed ratiometric fluorescence sensor can present a significant color change from red to green (inset in Fig. 2D), which is of great advantage for picking up colors with smartphones. Furthermore, as can be seen from fluorescence spectra of the PCDs, TPPS, and their mixture (Fig. 2D and S7), the PCDs and TPPS exhibit different emission at the same excitation wavelength of 400 nm. The emission peak of the PCDs at 510 nm exhibits a large difference of about 140 nm from the TPPS emission peak at 650 nm, which can effectively prevent the overlapping of the spectra with different emission peaks (Fig. 2D). In conclusion, the properties of both PCDs and TPPS can meet the requirements of a ratiometric fluorescence method for α-Glu sensing.

The experimental conditions including the reaction time, the concentration of α-arbutin, and the volume of EDA and TPPS were optimized to improve the sensing efficiency. The detailed descriptions and figures are shown in the electronic supplementary material (Fig. S8). The optimal reaction conditions were obtained as follows: reaction time of α-Glu and α-arbutin (30 min), reaction time of α-Glu-α-arbutin system and EDA (15 min), concentration of α-arbutin (0.2 mM), volume of EDA (6 μL, 12.5 M), and volume of TPPS (4 μL, 1 mM).

The sensitivity of the detection of α-Glu was evaluated under the above optimal experimental conditions. Firstly, the synthesized PCDs were employed for single-signal fluorescence detection of α-Glu. As shown in Fig. 3 A, the fluorescence of the sensing system at 510 nm is gradually increased with increasing α-Glu concentration from 0 to 0.04 U/mL, and the color of the solution changed from colorless to green under a 365 nm UV lamp (inset in Fig. 3A). Fig. 3B shows a good linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio (F/F 0, F 0 and F represent the fluorescence intensity of the reaction system before and after the addition of α-Glu, respectively) and the concentration of α-Glu in the range of 0.01–0.04 U/mL with R2 of 0.996. Based on the 3σ/slope rule, the limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 0.621 mU/mL. In addition, the corresponding color change is further verified using the CIE chromaticity coordinates, which shows a light to deep green trend as the α-Glu concentration increased from 0 to 0.04 U/mL (Fig. 3C).

Secondly, a ratiometric fluorescence pattern was developed after the addition of TPPS to the α-Glu detection system. When TPPS were added to the samples, two distinct peaks appeared in the fluorescence spectrum of the solution. As the concentration of α-Glu is gradually increased (0–0.04 U/mL), the fluorescence intensity is increased at 510 nm and decreased at 650 nm (Fig. 3D). Under 365 nm UV lamp irradiation, the solutions show a significant change from red to green, which can be clearly identified and distinguished by the naked eye (inset in Fig. 3D). As illustrated in Fig. 3E, the α-Glu concentration shows a well linear relationship with the fluorescence ratio (F 510/F 650, F 510 and F 650 represent the fluorescence intensity at the emission wavelengths of 510 nm and 650 nm, respectively) in the range of 0.01 to 0.04 U/mL (R2 = 0.997), and LOD values is as low as 0.184 mU/mL. Similarly, the trend in the color of the system was further verified using the CIE chromaticity diagram. As can be seen in Fig. 3F, the CIE coordinate diagram shows the trend of moving from red to green as the α-Glu concentration increases. The apparent color change of this sensing system provides the promise for development of a portable sensor for the detection of α-Glu. As can be seen from the linear range and LOD of the various nanomaterials for the determination of α-Glu (Table S1), the performance of this work is comparable or superior to most of the previously reported methods. The higher sensitivity of the ratiometric fluorescence method based on PCDs and TPPS compared to a single fluorescence signal response can be attributed to the self-correcting nature of ratiometric fluorescence, which effectively eliminates multiple background errors, allowing for more accurate analysis of targets in complex environments [46,47].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of the sensing platform for smartphone-assisted α-Glu detection
ce:section.ce:para: As the ratiometric fluorescence method described above, the sensing method requires relatively expensive and bulky instruments such as fluorescence spectrophotometers, which is not suitable for rapid and economic analysis in resource limited area. Also, visual analysis based on color changes by naked eye makes it difficult to quantify α-Glu concentrations. Therefore, to address these issues, the portable smartphone was used as a signal reader for the quantitative analysis of α-Glu. Considering the interference of ambient light on the detection, we designed a portable and simple detection dark box that combines it with a smartphone. As shown in Fig. S9, the dark box is made of black plastic polymerized materials to avoid interference from external light sources. Inside the dark box, a sample stage is set up with fixed grooves for placing samples. A 365 nm UV lamp bead with an illumination angle of 120° is fixed to the top flap of the dark box directly above the sample stage to provide the excitation light source. Outside the dark box, there is a specific slot for the common smartphone equipped with a camera and a black shading barrier next to the sample stage. Thus, the distance between the sample and UV lamp as well as between the sample and the smartphone is fixed. The detection light path is 90° from the incident light to avoid the interference of UV light source in the detection process, which largely reduces the error and ensures the accuracy of the detection. The specific operational steps are as follows.

First, ratiometric fluorescence sensing samples in the presence of different concentrations of α-Glu were sequentially placed into a 365 nm UV dark box as shown in Fig. 4 A. Secondly, the corresponding photographs were taken using a smartphone. Finally, the sample color signals were converted into color information (RGB values) by a color picking app (Swatches app) in the smartphone. We separately photographed sample solutions at α-Glu concentrations of 0–0.04 U/mL under 365 nm UV light irradiation (Fig. 4B). Combined with the Swatches app picking up the colors (Fig. 4B), the RGB values for the different concentrations of sample solutions were obtained (Fig. S10). As shown in Fig. 4C, the associated RGB ratio (R/B) shows linear correlation (R2 = 0.950) with α-Glu concentration over the range of 0.005 to 0.02 U/mL. To explore the reproducibility of the sensing platform, the detections of α-Glu were performed using two different smartphones, respectively. The obtained RSD between α-Glu concentrations with two smartphones were 1.03 %, suggesting satisfactory reproducibility of the smartphone-assisted α-Glu sensor. These results indicate that this portable smartphone-assisted sensor has great potential for in-situ, rapid detection.

The selectivity of the method was examined to ensure whether the ratiometric fluorescence system could be applied to the detection of α-Glu in real samples. Many common molecules (including some metal ions, sugars, amino acids, and enzymes) were detected. As shown in Fig. 5 A, the sensor shows extremely low optical response to analytes such as acetylcysteine (ATChE), glucose (Glc), glutamic acid (Glu), bovine serum protein (BSA), L-histidine (L-His), glutathione (GSH), Mg2+ and K+. In contrast, only the addition of α-Glu can cause a significantly higher fluorescence ratio (F 510/F 650). These data indicate that the constructed fluorescent probes show good selectivity for the detection of α-Glu. Therefore, it can be applied to the detection of α-Glu and screening AGIs in complex samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of α-Glu in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the practical value of the established method, different concentrations of α-Glu were added to diluted normal mouse serum (NMS) for validation. The mice blood was collected by eyeballs extraction, which was standing for 30 min at room temperature. The supernatant was taken after centrifuged at 3 500 r/min for 10 min. As can be seen in Fig. 5B, the fluorescence intensity at 510 nm is increased and the intensity at 650 nm is decreased as the α-Glu concentration gradually increased. There is a good linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio (F 510/F 650) and the α-Glu concentration (0.002–0.05 U/mL) with R2 of 0.998 (Fig. 5C). In addition, the efficiency and accuracy of the developed target detection method was further verified by a standard method, which utilized the GAA hydrolysis of p-nitrophenyl-α-D-glucopyranoside (pNPG) to generate p-nitrophenol (pNP) and then measured ultraviolet absorption at 400 nm of pNP. As shown in Fig. S11, the colorimetric method exhibits linear relationship between absorption and α-Glu concentrations in the range of 0.002–0.01 U/mL (R2 = 0.998). As shown in Table S2, the recoveries range of the proposed method and the standard method are in the range of 96 % to 105 % with the RSD less than 5 % when different concentrations of α-Glu are added to the NMS. The above results indicate that the interference factors in NMS are negligible in the designed method for α-Glu analysis and demonstrate the feasibility of the developed sensor for the accurate determination of α-Glu activity in various biological matrix samples.

AGIs play an important role in the therapy of diabetes. Therefore, it is very significant to establish efficient inhibitor screening methods. As previously reported, acarbose is a commonly used anti-diabetic drug that can precisely inhibit the catalytic activity of α-Glu [48]. Thus, acarbose was employed to investigate its inhibition on α-Glu activity based on the designed α-Glu assay. In this assay, α-Glu was incubated with acarbose prior to the addition of α-arbutin. As α-Glu activity was inhibited, the production of HQ was inhibited, and the production of PCDs was suppressed. Therefore, the fluorescence intensity is gradually decreased at 510 nm and increased at 650 nm with the increase of acarbose concentration (Fig. 5D and E). As illustrated in Fig. 5F, the classical S-type calibration curve is obtained by plotting the inhibition rate versus acarbose concentration. The IC50 value (concentration of inhibitor when the inhibition rate is 50 % [34]) is calculated to be 3.497 mM based on the S-type calibration curve, which is similar to the previously reported method [49]. In addition, natural products (such as rhodiola rosea) have been reported to be potential AGIs [50–53]. Therefore, the inhibition ability of rhodiola rosea, and its active ingredients (such as gallic acid, quercetin, and epicatechin) on α-Glu activity were separately investigated based on the designed method. As shown in Fig. S12, the crude extract of rhodiola rosea, gallic acid, quercetin, and epicatechin can inhibit the activity of α-Glu to some extent. In addition, the fluorescence spectra of synthesized PCDs and TPPS system were detected before and after the addition of gallic acid, quercetin, and epicatechin. As shown in Fig. S13, the fluorescence of PCDs and TPPS system are barely quenched by these ingredients, which further confirm their inhibition of α-Glu activity. The above results indicate that the constructed ratiometric fluorescent sensor has the potential to screen AGIs from complex natural products.

In summary, a smartphone-assisted ratiometric fluorescence sensor has been successfully established for detecting the α-Glu activity and screening AGIs. Based on α-Glu catalyzed the production of HQ, the green fluorescent PCDs were synthesized in-situ under a mild condition. With introduction of red fluorescent TPPS, the ratiometric fluorescence method was designed for α-Glu activity detection and AGIs screening. The ratiometric fluorometric method has good linearity in the range of 0.01–0.04 U/mL and the LOD is as low as 0.184 mU/mL. The developed sensor exhibit good selectivity, and can be applied for α-Glu activity detection in complex biological samples and AGIs screening in natural products. The whole process is simple to perform and the experimental material is cheap and easy to obtain. Due to the advantages of high sensitivity, portability, and easy signal reading, the smartphone-assisted ratiometric fluorescence method has great potential application in the rapid detection of α-Glu activity and high-thought screening of AGIs in resource-limited areas.

Hui-Ting Cao: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Tiansheng Zhao: Data curation, Writing – original draft. Wei Liu: Investigation, Visualization. Cai-Ying Xu: Data curation, Investigation. Yi-Jing Liao: Resources, Supervision. Xi-Luan Yan: Methodology, Supervision. Xi Mai: Funding acquisition, Resources, Writing – review & editing. Na Li: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2023.109723.
